---
title: "TransferLearningExample_v2"
output: html_document
date: "2025-09-04"
---


# Title: Transfer Learning for Disease Prediction (Robust Setup)
# Description: This script uses a more robust, multi-step process to set up the
#              Python environment, designed for clean R/RStudio installations.
# Author: Gemini
# Date: September 4, 2025


```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{r}
library(tidyselect)
library(dplyr)
```


```{r Install Proper packages}

# --- 1. SETUP ---
# This section will automatically install the required R packages and then
# perform a robust, multi-step setup of the Python environment.

# Install R packages if they are not already present
if (!require("keras")) install.packages("keras")
if (!require("dplyr")) install.packages("dplyr")
if (!require("ggplot2")) install.packages("ggplot2")

# Load the necessary libraries.
library(keras)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# This block performs a more robust, multi-step installation of Keras/TensorFlow.
if (!keras::is_keras_available()) {
  cat("--- Keras/Python environment not found. Starting robust setup process. ---\n")

  # Step 1: Install Miniconda if it's not found
  # reticulate will manage a local Python installation just for R.
  # If prompted to install, please type 'Y' (yes).
  if (is.null(tryCatch(reticulate::miniconda_path(), error = function(e) NULL))) {
    cat("Step 1: Installing Miniconda...\n")
    reticulate::install_miniconda()
  }

  # Step 2: Create a dedicated Conda environment
  env_name <- "r-keras-clean-env"
  cat("Step 2: Creating a new Conda environment named '", env_name, "'...\n", sep = "")
  # Check if it already exists from a failed attempt, and remove if so.
  if (env_name %in% reticulate::conda_list()$name) {
    reticulate::conda_remove(env_name)
  }
  # We specify a stable Python version for better compatibility.
  reticulate::conda_create(envname = env_name, python_version = "3.9")

  # Step 3: Install Python packages into the new environment using Conda
  cat("Step 3: Installing Python packages (TensorFlow, Keras, etc.)...\n")
  cat("This will take several minutes.\n")
  keras::install_keras(
    method = "conda",
    # FIX: Use conda_binary("auto") to find the executable instead of the directory
    conda = reticulate::conda_binary("auto"),
    envname = env_name,
    tensorflow = "2.16.1" # Specify a modern, compatible version
  )

  # Step 4: Tell reticulate to use this environment for the current session
  reticulate::use_condaenv(env_name, required = TRUE)
  cat("\n--- Setup Complete ---\n")
  cat("ACTION: Please restart your R session ('Session' -> 'Restart R') for the changes to take effect.\n")
  stop("Restart required.") # Stop script to force restart
}


# Final verification after restart
if (interactive() && !keras::is_keras_available()) {
   stop("Keras installation failed. This may be due to missing system dependencies. Please ensure Xcode Command Line Tools are installed by running 'xcode-select --install' in your Mac's Terminal.")
} else {
  cat("Keras is ready to use.\n\n")
}
```


```{r }
# --- 2. DATA SIMULATION ---
# We'll create two datasets to mimic your scenario.
set.seed(42) # for reproducibility

# Dataset 2: General Population (large, high-quality features)
n_large <- 5000
n_features_large <- 20
df.2 <- data.frame(replicate(n_features_large, rnorm(n_large)))
true_coeffs_large <- runif(n_features_large, -2, 2)
p.2 <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(as.matrix(df.2) %*% true_coeffs_large + rnorm(n_large, 0, 1))))
df.2$Dz <- runif(n_large) < p.2

# Dataset 1: Specific Population (smaller, subset of features)
n_small <- 200
n_features_small <- 5
df.1 <- data.frame(replicate(n_features_small, rnorm(n_small, mean = 0.5, sd = 1.2)))
colnames(df.1) <- paste0("X", 1:n_features_small)
true_coeffs_small <- true_coeffs_large[1:n_features_small]
p.1 <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(as.matrix(df.1) %*% true_coeffs_small + rnorm(n_small, 0.5, 1.5))))
df.1$Dz <- runif(n_small) < p.1

cat(sprintf("Dataset 2 (General): %d samples, %d features\n", nrow(df.2), ncol(df.2)-1))
cat(sprintf("Dataset 1 (Specific): %d samples, %d features\n\n", nrow(df.1), ncol(df.1)-1))


# --- 3. DATA PREPARATION ---
x.2 <- as.matrix(df.2 %>% select(-Dz))
y.2 <- as.numeric(df.2$Dz)
x.2_scaled <- scale(x.2)

x.1 <- as.matrix(df.1 %>% select(-Dz))
y.1 <- as.numeric(df.1$Dz)
x.1_scaled <- scale(x.1)


# Title: Transfer Learning for Disease Prediction (Robust Setup)
# Description: This script uses a more robust, multi-step process to set up the
#              Python environment, designed for clean R/RStudio installations.
# Author: Gemini
# Date: September 4, 2025

# --- 1. SETUP ---
# This section will automatically install the required R packages and then
# perform a robust, multi-step setup of the Python environment.

# Install R packages if they are not already present
if (!require("keras")) install.packages("keras")
if (!require("dplyr")) install.packages("dplyr")
if (!require("ggplot2")) install.packages("ggplot2")

# Load the necessary libraries.
library(keras)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# This block performs a more robust, multi-step installation of Keras/TensorFlow.
if (!keras::is_keras_available()) {
  cat("--- Keras/Python environment not found. Starting robust setup process. ---\n")

  # Step 1: Install Miniconda if it's not found
  # reticulate will manage a local Python installation just for R.
  # If prompted to install, please type 'Y' (yes).
  if (is.null(tryCatch(reticulate::miniconda_path(), error = function(e) NULL))) {
    cat("Step 1: Installing Miniconda...\n")
    reticulate::install_miniconda()
  }

  # Step 2: Create a dedicated Conda environment
  env_name <- "r-keras-clean-env"
  cat("Step 2: Creating a new Conda environment named '", env_name, "'...\n", sep = "")
  # Check if it already exists from a failed attempt, and remove if so.
  if (env_name %in% reticulate::conda_list()$name) {
    reticulate::conda_remove(env_name)
  }
  # We specify a stable Python version for better compatibility.
  reticulate::conda_create(envname = env_name, python_version = "3.9")

  # Step 3: Install Python packages into the new environment using Conda
  cat("Step 3: Installing Python packages (TensorFlow, Keras, etc.)...\n")
  cat("This will take several minutes.\n")
  keras::install_keras(
    method = "conda",
    conda = reticulate::conda_binary("auto"),
    envname = env_name,
    tensorflow = "2.16.1" # Specify a modern, compatible version
  )

  # Step 4: Tell reticulate to use this environment for the current session
  reticulate::use_condaenv(env_name, required = TRUE)
  cat("\n--- Setup Complete ---\n")
  cat("ACTION: Please restart your R session ('Session' -> 'Restart R') for the changes to take effect.\n")
  stop("Restart required.") # Stop script to force restart
}


# Final verification after restart
if (interactive() && !keras::is_keras_available()) {
   stop("Keras installation failed. This may be due to missing system dependencies. Please ensure Xcode Command Line Tools are installed by running 'xcode-select --install' in your Mac's Terminal.")
} else {
  cat("Keras is ready to use.\n\n")
}


# --- 2. DATA SIMULATION ---
# We'll create two datasets to mimic your scenario.
set.seed(42) # for reproducibility

# Dataset 2: General Population (large, high-quality features)
n_large <- 5000
n_features_large <- 20
df.2 <- data.frame(replicate(n_features_large, rnorm(n_large)))
true_coeffs_large <- runif(n_features_large, -2, 2)
p.2 <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(as.matrix(df.2) %*% true_coeffs_large + rnorm(n_large, 0, 1))))
df.2$Dz <- runif(n_large) < p.2

# Dataset 1: Specific Population (smaller, subset of features)
n_small <- 200
n_features_small <- 5
df.1 <- data.frame(replicate(n_features_small, rnorm(n_small, mean = 0.5, sd = 1.2)))
colnames(df.1) <- paste0("X", 1:n_features_small)
true_coeffs_small <- true_coeffs_large[1:n_features_small]
p.1 <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(as.matrix(df.1) %*% true_coeffs_small + rnorm(n_small, 0.5, 1.5))))
df.1$Dz <- runif(n_small) < p.1

cat(sprintf("Dataset 2 (General): %d samples, %d features\n", nrow(df.2), ncol(df.2)-1))
cat(sprintf("Dataset 1 (Specific): %d samples, %d features\n\n", nrow(df.1), ncol(df.1)-1))


# --- 3. DATA PREPARATION ---
x.2 <- as.matrix(df.2 %>% select(-Dz))
y.2 <- as.numeric(df.2$Dz)
x.2_scaled <- scale(x.2)

x.1 <- as.matrix(df.1 %>% select(-Dz))
y.1 <- as.numeric(df.1$Dz)
x.1_scaled <- scale(x.1)


# --- 4. MODELING: PRE-TRAINING & FINE-TUNING ---

build_model <- function(input_shape) {
  # Define the model layers first, using the keras:: prefix to be explicit
  model <- keras::keras_model_sequential() %>%
    keras::layer_dense(units = 32, activation = "relu", input_shape = input_shape) %>%
    keras::layer_dropout(rate = 0.3) %>%
    keras::layer_dense(units = 16, activation = "relu") %>%
    keras::layer_dropout(rate = 0.2) %>%
    keras::layer_dense(units = 1, activation = "sigmoid")

  # Then, compile the model object directly. This is more robust.
  keras::compile(model,
    loss = "binary_crossentropy",
    optimizer = "adam",
    metrics = "accuracy"
  )

  return(model)
}

cat("--- Training Model from Scratch on Small Dataset (df.1) ---\n")
scratch_model <- build_model(input_shape = ncol(x.1_scaled))
# FIX: Use keras::fit() to be explicit
history_scratch <- keras::fit(
  scratch_model,
  x.1_scaled,
  y.1,
  epochs = 50,
  batch_size = 16,
  validation_split = 0.2,
  verbose = 0
)
# FIX: Use keras::evaluate() to be explicit
scratch_eval <- keras::evaluate(scratch_model, x.1_scaled, y.1, verbose = 0)
cat(sprintf("Model (from Scratch) Final Accuracy on df.1: %.2f%%\n", scratch_eval[["accuracy"]] * 100))

cat("\n--- Pre-training a base model on related features from df.2 ---\n")
base_model_for_transfer <- build_model(input_shape = ncol(x.1_scaled))
# FIX: Use keras::fit() to be explicit
history_base <- keras::fit(
  base_model_for_transfer,
  x.2_scaled[, 1:ncol(x.1_scaled)],
  y.2,
  epochs = 20,
  batch_size = 128,
  validation_split = 0.2,
  verbose = 0
)

cat("\n--- Fine-tuning the pre-trained model on Small Dataset (df.1) ---\n")
finetune_model <- base_model_for_transfer

# Compile the model object directly instead of using the pipe.
keras::compile(finetune_model,
    loss = "binary_crossentropy",
    optimizer = keras::optimizer_adam(learning_rate = 0.0001),
    metrics = "accuracy"
)

# FIX: Use keras::fit() to be explicit
history_finetune <- keras::fit(
  finetune_model,
  x.1_scaled,
  y.1,
  epochs = 50,
  batch_size = 16,
  validation_split = 0.2,
  verbose = 0
)
# FIX: Use keras::evaluate() to be explicit
finetune_eval <- keras::evaluate(finetune_model, x.1_scaled, y.1, verbose = 0)
cat(sprintf("Model (Fine-Tuned) Final Accuracy on df.1: %.2f%%\n\n", finetune_eval[["accuracy"]] * 100))


# --- 5. VISUALIZE RESULTS ---
results <- data.frame(
  epoch = 1:50,
  scratch_val_acc = history_scratch$metrics$val_accuracy,
  finetune_val_acc = history_finetune$metrics$val_accuracy
)

ggplot(results, aes(x = epoch)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = scratch_val_acc, color = "Trained from Scratch")) +
  geom_line(aes(y = finetune_val_acc, color = "Fine-Tuned (Transfer Learning)")) +
  labs(
    title = "Transfer Learning vs. Training from Scratch",
    subtitle = "Validation accuracy on the small, specific dataset (df.1)",
    y = "Validation Accuracy",
    x = "Epoch",
    color = "Model Type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent)
```
